title: Magnesium foot soak
subtitle: An Epsom salt foot soak with dried peppermint leaves for tired feet
excerpt: A simple bulk-mix foot soak of Epsom salts, bicarbonate of soda, and dried peppermint leaves stored in a kilner jar. Scoop a handful into a bowl of warm water for a 15-minute soak. The bicarbonate softens the water; the peppermint contributes a faint cooling note as the leaves steep.
sourceNotes: Bulk-mix bath salt and foot soak ratios per standard formulating practice: Epsom salts 70-80%, bicarbonate of soda 15-20%, dried botanical 5-10%. Peppermint leaves steep at bath temperature without releasing significant essential oil; the cooling is mild compared to peppermint EO at the same dose.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 500 ml kilner jar (~500 g; about 8 soaks at 60 g per soak)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale up for larger jars. A 1 L kilner takes a 1 kg batch and lasts a month at one soak every other day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps 12 months sealed in a dry jar. The peppermint leaves fade in colour and aroma over months; the salt is stable indefinitely.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months sealed in a dry jar. The mix clumps if it gets damp; keep the jar out of the steamy zone of the bathroom. Discard if mould appears on the dried leaves (won't happen if the jar stayed dry; will happen fast if it didn't).

glossaryTerms.term: Epsom salt in a foot soak
glossaryTerms.definition: Epsom salts (magnesium sulphate) dissolved in warm water create an osmotic gradient against the skin. The traditional claim of magnesium absorption through skin is unsupported by good evidence, but the warm hypertonic soak does reduce mild foot swelling and the salt makes the water feel softer. Use 50-100 g per litre of soak water.

content.text: A foot soak is a homely ritual after a long day on hard floors. The

is the workhorse; the bicarbonate softens the water; the dried peppermint leaves steep faintly as the bowl cools. The mix lives in a kilner jar by the bath and a handful goes into a basin or footbath.

This is a bulk-mix recipe rather than a single dose. The yield makes about 8 soaks at 60 g per soak. The water temperature matters more than the salt amount: comfortable warm, not hot. A 15-20 minute soak is the practical sweet spot.

items.prepNote: Plain Epsom salts. Bath-grade or food-grade both work; check the label is sodium-free magnesium sulphate.
Plain bicarbonate of soda (baking soda). Softens the soak water and helps remove rough skin.
Whole dried peppermint leaves, not crushed. Whole leaves don't sink to the bottom of the bowl the way crushed do.

content.text: Weigh the Epsom salts and bicarbonate of soda into the mixing bowl. Add the dried peppermint leaves. Stir thoroughly with a spoon until evenly distributed. Funnel into a clean dry 500 ml kilner jar; clip closed; label with the date. Keep the jar by the bath but out of the steam zone (above the towel rail or in a closed cupboard).

Fill a basin or footbath with comfortably warm water (40 degrees Celsius is a good guide; not hot). Scoop a heaped handful (about 60 g) of the mix into the water. Stir to dissolve the salt; the leaves will float. Soak feet for 15-20 minutes. Pat dry with a towel; the salt residue rinses off in a quick foot-rinse if it feels gritty. Follow with a body butter on the feet if you want a moisturising finish.
Keep the jar dry. The mix lasts 12 months sealed; the peppermint leaves fade slowly. If the mix clumps from humidity, break up with a fork before scooping. Discard if mould develops on the leaves.

Skip if you have broken skin, open blisters, or diabetic foot complications - the salt sting is unpleasant on broken skin and a warm soak can mask early signs of trouble on diabetic feet. Check water temperature with a hand first if you have any peripheral neuropathy. Drink water during the soak; warm soaks are mildly dehydrating.
